attributes.stem:
A 12-year-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic with her parent concerned about brown spots on her face and arms that become more noticeable in summer. She has dark skin and black hair. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. The lesions were not present at birth and her uncle was recently diagnosed with melanoma at age 45.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple small (2-3 mm), light brown, uniform macules scattered across her cheeks, nose, and forearms. The macules are flat and non-palpable with regular borders. The lesions are more prominent on sun-exposed areas.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of ephelides (freckles): small, light brown macules that become more prominent with sun exposure. The age of onset in childhood and the characteristic distribution on sun-exposed areas are typical features.

Ephelides represent increased melanin production within normal numbers of melanocytes. They typically appear in childhood, darken with sun exposure, and fade in winter. They are genetic in nature and are associated with variants in the melanocortin-1 receptor (MC1R) gene, which is also responsible for red hair and fair skin. They are more common in fair skinned people however can still occur in other skin types.

Solar lentigo (also known as age spots or liver spots) typically appears in older adults as a result of chronic sun exposure. They do not fade in winter and are larger and darker than ephelides. They represent both increased melanin and increased numbers of melanocytes. It is unlikely diagnosis due to her age and presentation.

Melanocytic naevi are usually raised, darker, and more uniform in colour. They can be present at birth or appear throughout life but do not show the characteristic seasonal variation in appearance seen with ephelides.

Café-au-lait macules are larger (>5 mm), light brown patches that are uniform in colour and do not vary with sun exposure. Multiple café-au-lait macules can be associated with neurofibromatosis type 1.

Lentigo simplex appears as darker brown macules that do not vary with sun exposure. They can occur anywhere on the body, including non-sun-exposed areas, and are not typically associated with fair skin and red hair.
learningPoints: Ephelides (freckles) are small, light brown macules that darken with sun exposure and typically appear in childhood.
Ephelides are more common in fair-skinned individuals and represent increased melanin production rather than increased melanocyte numbers.
Distribution pattern and age of onset are key distinguishing features between ephelides and other pigmented lesions.
references: Ngan V, Oakley A. Lentigines. DermNet NZ. 2016. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/lentigo
Lodish MB, Stratakis CA. The differential diagnosis of familial lentiginosis syndromes. Fam Cancer. 2011 Sep;10(3):481-90. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3417307/
